intro: Python offers high-level abstraction and productivity through dynamic typing, whereas C delivers bare-metal control, deterministic memory management, and exceptional execution speed. Developers port Python code to C to optimize performance-critical hotspots, eliminate garbage collection pauses, or target constrained embedded hardware. This transition exchanges developer velocity and rich high-level data models for explicit memory operations and manual pointer manipulation.

diffs.from: Dynamically and strongly typed. Type checks occur at runtime, and objects contain extensive metadata headers.
diffs.to: Statically and weakly typed. Types are verified during compilation, allowing arbitrary memory reinterpretations via pointers and casts.

diffs.from: Indentation-defined blocks, minimal boilerplate, and high-level expression constructs like list comprehensions.
diffs.to: Brace-delimited statements, mandatory semicolons, and explicit declarations for all variables, loops, and memory references.

diffs.from: Managed automatically via reference counting combined with an automatic cyclic garbage collector.
diffs.to: Manual memory management. The developer explicitly calls malloc/calloc to allocate heap space and free to prevent resource leaks.

diffs.from: Native asynchronous concurrency supported by async/await syntax and managed by an internal event loop engine.
diffs.to: No native async syntax. Concurrency requires direct OS-level threading models (pthreads), low-level multiplexing (select/poll), or third-party event libraries.

diffs.from: Extensive standard library including high-level data types, networking protocols, crypto, and OS abstractions out of the box.
diffs.to: Minimalist library focusing primarily on basic I/O, string manipulation, mathematical operations, and basic system calls.

examples.title: Summing elements of an array
examples.description: Compares high-level iteration over a list to a contiguous memory loop with explicit sizing.

examples.title: Writing data safely to a text file
examples.description: Illustrates Python's context manager against manual file descriptor opening, writing, and closure.
examples.fromCode:
with open('out.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Hello World\n')

tips.title: Always Validate Buffer Boundaries
tips.body: C strings do not track their length and rely on null terminators. Ensure target allocations have adequate capacity to prevent buffer overflow vulnerabilities.

tips.title: Match Every Allocation with Free
tips.body: To prevent memory exhaustion, every call to malloc, calloc, or realloc must have a corresponding, reachable path to a free statement.

tips.title: Reconstruct High-Level Structs
tips.body: Python dicts and lists must be rewritten using custom C structs, linked lists, or hash tables. Consider using tested open-source collections.

tips.title: Replace Dynamic Types with Enums and Unions
tips.body: To handle polymorphic structures, combine an explicit enum type flag with a union fields construct inside a parent struct container.

faqs.question: How do I replicate Python's dynamic list resizing in C?
faqs.answer: You must implement a dynamic array structure. This involves tracking an array pointer, its current item count, and its max capacity, then manually expanding it via `realloc` when capacity is exceeded.

faqs.question: Can I invoke C code directly within my Python codebase?
faqs.answer: Yes, using the `ctypes` library, `cffi`, or by writing native Python C extensions, you can load compiled `.so` or `.dll` C binaries and execute them for speed optimizations.

faqs.question: What happens if I forget to free memory in C?
faqs.answer: Unlike Python, which cleans up unreferenced objects via garbage collection, C will retain that memory allocation until the host process terminates, causing a memory leak.
